questions.question: A robotics firm can produce 60 industrial arms or 15 autonomous delivery bots per month. They shift resources to produce 5 more delivery bots. How many industrial arms must they sacrifice?
options: 5 industrial arms, matching the increase in delivery bot production output
20 industrial arms, reflecting the trade-off ratio of four arms per delivery bot
15 industrial arms, equal to the total monthly delivery bot production capacity
60 industrial arms, representing the entire industrial arm manufacturing line
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 delivery bot = $60 \div 15 = 4$ industrial arms. For 5 additional bots: $5 \times 4 = 20$ industrial arms sacrificed.

questions.question: Country North produces a computer in 20 hours and a car in 80 hours. Country South produces a computer in 25 hours and a car in 100 hours. Which statement about absolute advantage is true?
options: Country South has the absolute advantage because its production times are closer together
Country North has an absolute advantage in both computers and car production
Country South has the absolute advantage in cars because it uses more total labor hours
Neither country has an absolute advantage because both produce both goods
questions.explanation: Country North needs fewer hours for computers ($20 < 25$) and cars ($80 < 100$), giving it the absolute advantage in both goods.

questions.question: Using the same data (North: 20h/Computer, 80h/Car; South: 25h/Computer, 100h/Car), which country has the comparative advantage in cars?
options: Country North, because it produces cars using fewer total labor hours
Neither country, because both have identical opportunity costs of 4 computers per car
Country South, because it has the higher absolute labor requirements for both goods
Country North, because its car production is four times its computer production time
questions.explanation: North's cost of 1 car = $80/20 = 4$ computers. South's cost = $100/25 = 4$ computers. Identical ratios mean no comparative advantage exists.

questions.question: What does a Production Possibility Frontier that is bowed outward from the origin imply about the economy's resources?
options: All resources are perfectly adaptable and can be used equally well in any industry
Resources are specialized and not equally suited to producing every type of good
The economy is operating at a point inside its production frontier boundary
Production can expand indefinitely without any opportunity cost or trade-off
questions.explanation: A concave PPF reflects increasing opportunity costs. Resources are specialized, so shifting production requires increasingly larger sacrifices.

questions.question: Under autarky, Country X's internal cost of Good A is 0.5 units of Good B. The world price of Good A is 0.7 units of Good B. What should Country X do when trade opens?
options: Import Good A because the world price is higher than the domestic cost of production
Export Good A because the world price exceeds Country X's domestic opportunity cost
Remain in autarky because the price difference is too small to generate any gains
Export Good B because the world price of Good A is unfavorable for Country X
questions.explanation: Country X's internal cost of A ($0.5$ B) is lower than the world price ($0.7$ B). Country X has a comparative advantage in A and should export it.

questions.question: On the World Relative Supply curve, what determines the vertical height of the first horizontal step?
options: The average of the two trading countries' domestic opportunity cost ratios
The opportunity cost of the good in the country that possesses the comparative advantage
The world equilibrium price determined by the intersection with relative demand
The autarky price ratio of the larger of the two trading partner countries
questions.explanation: The first step begins at the lowest price at which any country is willing to supply the good, defined by the most efficient producer's internal opportunity cost.

questions.question: The labor endowment of the country with the comparative advantage in Good X doubles. What happens to the World Relative Supply curve?
options: The first step shifts upward because more labor increases the opportunity cost
The horizontal length of the first step doubles as maximum potential output expands
The curve becomes perfectly elastic at the new labor-adjusted equilibrium price
The curve is unaffected because relative supply depends on prices, not labor supply
questions.explanation: More labor in the specializing country increases the total quantity of Good X that can be supplied at its specific opportunity cost, lengthening the step.

questions.question: Home wage is \$25 and Foreign wage is \$10. Home productivity in Good Z is 5 units per hour. What must Foreign productivity be for unit labor costs to be equal in both countries?
options: 10, so that Foreign's total output matches its wage rate of \$10 per hour
2, because \$10 divided by productivity must equal Home's unit cost of \$5
5, matching Home's productivity level to achieve identical unit labor costs
2.5, the midpoint between Home productivity and the Foreign wage rate
questions.explanation: Home unit labor cost = $\$25/5 = \$5$. For Foreign to match: $\$10 / \text{Productivity} = \$5$, so Productivity must be $2$.

questions.question: Which common misconception about international trade and wages does the Ricardian model successfully debunk?
options: Trade always reduces wages in every country that participates in it
A country can only compete internationally if its wages are lower than its neighbors' wages
Free trade guarantees that wages will equalize across every trading nation
Wages have no relationship whatsoever to productivity in the Ricardian framework
questions.explanation: The Ricardian model shows high-wage countries can compete successfully when their productivity is sufficiently high to offset the wage difference.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, trade is described as mutually beneficial because it enables countries to do what?
options: Eliminate all domestic production and rely entirely on imported foreign goods
Indirectly produce goods more efficiently through exchange than through domestic production
Achieve complete equality of wages and living standards across all trading partners
Avoid the need for any domestic labor by importing goods from lower-wage countries
questions.explanation: Trade acts as a superior technology that transforms exports into imports at a better exchange rate than domestic production allows.

questions.question: A country has a workforce of 100, $a_{LX} = 2$, and $a_{LY} = 4$. What is the maximum amount of Good Y it can consume under autarky?
options: 50 units, calculated by dividing the total labor force by $a_{LX}$
25 units, equal to total available labor divided by the unit labor requirement for Y
100 units, matching the total size of the country's available workforce
200 units, the product of the total labor force and the labor requirement
questions.explanation: Maximum Good Y = Total Labor $\div a_{LY} = 100 \div 4 = 25$ units of Good Y.

questions.question: Why does a transition from autarky to free trade represent a potential Pareto Improvement rather than an automatic one?
options: Because trade benefits are purely theoretical and never occur in the real world
Aggregate consumption possibilities expand, but some individuals may still be made worse off
Free trade always makes every single citizen better off without any exceptions
Pareto Improvements require government approval before they can be officially recognized
questions.explanation: The total pool of goods grows, so winners could theoretically compensate losers. Without actual compensation, however, some individuals are worse off.

questions.question: An economy has perfectly competitive markets throughout and no externalities are present anywhere. What does the First Welfare Theorem predict about the outcome?
options: Government intervention is absolutely required to achieve an efficient allocation
The market equilibrium will be Pareto Efficient, exhausting all possible trade gains
The outcome will be equitable but not necessarily efficient in production or exchange
Market power will emerge and prevent the economy from reaching any equilibrium
questions.explanation: The First Welfare Theorem states that perfectly competitive markets with no externalities produce Pareto Efficient outcomes.

questions.question: In a Ricardian economy, $P_X = 100$, $P_Y = 200$, $a_{LX} = 2$, and $a_{LY} = 5$. Where will the labor force be employed?
options: Split equally between the two sectors because both are profitable to operate
Entirely in Sector X, where the hourly wage of \$50 exceeds Sector Y's wage of \$40
Entirely in Sector Y, because the price per unit of Good Y is twice as high as Good X
Workers will be indifferent because total revenue is equal in both sectors
questions.explanation: Wage in X = $100/2 = \$50$. Wage in Y = $200/5 = \$40$. All labor migrates to the higher-paying Sector X.

questions.question: What does the assumption of perfect labor mobility in the Ricardian model imply about its time frame?
options: It is a short-run model where workers cannot change industries quickly
It is a long-run model where sufficient time exists for retraining and relocation
It is a static model that makes no predictions about any time horizon at all
It applies only to the very short run before any adjustments can take place
questions.explanation: Perfect mobility assumes workers have had enough time to retrain and move freely between different industries in response to wage differentials.

questions.question: Unit labor requirements are $a_{LX} = 1$ and $a_{LY} = 3$. The world relative price $P_X/P_Y$ is 0.5. What will this country produce?
options: Only Good Y, because its internal cost of Y is lower than the world price
Only Good X, because the world price of X exceeds its domestic opportunity cost
Both goods equally, splitting the labor force between the two sectors
Neither good, because the world price equals the domestic opportunity cost
questions.explanation: Internal opportunity cost of X = $a_{LX}/a_{LY} = 1/3 \approx 0.33$. World price of X ($0.5$) exceeds domestic cost, so the country specializes in and exports X.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the price of an import-competing good suddenly drops. Who suffers the losses in that industry?
options: Only the owners of capital, because labor can quickly move to the export sector
Both labor and capital owners together, because neither factor can leave the declining industry
Only foreign exporters who now receive a lower price for their goods abroad
No one suffers because lower prices benefit everyone in the domestic economy
questions.explanation: Because factors cannot leave the industry, both workers and capital owners must accept lower wages and rental rates as the sector's total revenue falls.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, which trade policy would a worker employed in the import-competing sector most strongly support?
options: An export subsidy that helps firms in other industries sell more abroad
A high import tariff that raises the domestic price of the good they produce
Free trade, because it lowers the prices of all consumer goods for workers
A voluntary export restraint negotiated with the principal foreign supplier
questions.explanation: A tariff raises the domestic price of their industry's output, directly protecting the nominal and real income of workers trapped in that sector.

questions.question: How does the immobile factor model differ fundamentally from the Specific Factor model?
options: The immobile model assumes free trade while the Specific Factor model assumes autarky
In the immobile model, labor is also stuck in its industry rather than being mobile between sectors
The Specific Factor model has three factors while the immobile model has only two factors
There is no real difference because both models describe the same economic scenario
questions.explanation: The immobile factor model is a more extreme short-run case where no factors of production, including labor, can reallocate between different industries.

questions.question: Country A has a Capital-to-Labor ratio of 0.8 and Country B has a ratio of 0.5. According to the H-O definition, which country is capital-abundant?
options: Country B, because a lower ratio indicates that capital is relatively more scarce
Country A, because it has a higher ratio of capital per worker than Country B has
Country B, because capital abundance is determined by the absolute stock of capital
Neither country, because both ratios are below 1.0 and capital is scarce in both
questions.explanation: Factor abundance is a relative measure. Country A's K/L ratio ($0.8$) exceeds Country B's ratio ($0.5$), making Country A relatively capital-abundant.

questions.question: According to the Stolper-Samuelson Theorem, an increase in the price of a labor-intensive good will have what distributional effect?
options: Both real wages and real capital rents will increase by exactly the same percentage
Real wages of labor will rise while the real rental rate of capital will decrease
Only capital owners will benefit while labor's real wage remains completely unchanged
The theorem makes no prediction about the relationship between goods and factor prices
questions.explanation: The factor used intensively in the industry whose price rises gains in real purchasing power. The other factor experiences a decline in its real return.

questions.question: In the H-O model, the relative wage ($w/r$) increases. How does the capital-to-labor ratio ($K/L$) respond in both industries?
options: The $K/L$ ratio falls as firms hire more workers to take advantage of lower wages
The $K/L$ ratio rises in both industries as firms substitute capital for costlier labor
The $K/L$ ratio rises in labor-intensive sectors but falls in capital-intensive ones
The $K/L$ ratio is technologically fixed and cannot change regardless of factor prices
questions.explanation: As labor becomes relatively more expensive, profit-maximizing firms in every sector substitute away from labor toward capital, raising $K/L$ ratios throughout the economy.

questions.question: What is the primary conclusion of the Rybczynski Theorem in the Heckscher-Ohlin framework?
options: An increase in both factors leads to proportional output growth in every industry
Expanding one factor raises output of the good using it intensively while the other sector contracts
Factor endowments have no effect on the production mix when goods prices are held constant
Only technological progress, not factor accumulation, can change the composition of output
questions.explanation: At constant goods prices, increasing one factor endowment expands the sector using that factor intensively and actually reduces output in the other sector.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, an increase in the price of the export good ($P_X$) causes labor to reallocate in which direction?
options: Labor moves from the export sector to the import-competing sector to balance growth
Labor shifts from the import-competing sector into the now higher-paying export sector
Labor allocation remains unchanged because specific factors determine employment levels
Labor exits both sectors and moves into non-traded domestic service industries
questions.explanation: Higher export prices increase the Value of Marginal Product of Labor in the export sector. Workers are drawn away from the import-competing sector.

questions.question: The specific factor in Manufacturing (Capital) increases in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the return to Land in Agriculture?
options: The return to land rises because a stronger manufacturing sector benefits everyone
The return to land decreases as higher wages reduce the surplus available for landowners
The return to land remains constant because land and factories are separate sectors
The return to land rises in the short run but returns to normal in the long run
questions.explanation: More capital raises labor demand in manufacturing, bidding up the economy-wide wage. Higher wages in agriculture reduce the residual surplus left for landowners.

questions.question: The Specific Factor Model is best described as a model of which time horizon in economic analysis?
options: The very short run, where no factors of production can adjust to any changes
The medium run, where labor is mobile but capital and land remain fixed in their sectors
The very long run, where all factors of production are perfectly mobile across sectors
A static framework that makes no distinction between different time horizons at all
questions.explanation: The model captures an intermediate timeframe: labor has adjusted to changed conditions, but capital and land remain specific to their current industries.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of Manufacturing goods falls by 5%. By how much does the real income of Manufacturing capital owners change?
options: It falls by exactly 5%, matching the percentage decrease in the output price
It falls by more than 5%, amplified by the Magnification Effect on specific factor returns
It falls by less than 5%, cushioned by labor moving out of the manufacturing sector
It remains unchanged because capital returns are determined solely by technology
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect means specific factor returns change more than proportionally to output price changes. A 5% price decline causes an even larger fall in capital's real return.

questions.question: Which assumption is fundamental to the Krugman model of monopolistic competition in international trade?
options: All firms produce perfectly identical homogeneous products with no differentiation
Firms produce differentiated products and operate under internal economies of scale
A single monopoly firm dominates the entire global market for each product category
External economies of scale cause industries to cluster in specific geographic locations
questions.explanation: Product differentiation gives each firm some pricing power, while internal economies of scale mean larger output lowers average production costs.

questions.question: What is the market size effect described in New Trade Theory?
options: Smaller markets are always more efficient because they have less wasteful competition
A larger integrated market enables more firms to exist while each produces at lower average cost
Market size has no effect on the number of firms or their production cost levels
Larger markets always lead to monopoly because only the biggest firm can survive
questions.explanation: Trade expands the effective market size. This allows for greater product variety and lets each firm produce more, spreading fixed costs over larger output.

questions.question: When an industry has external economies of scale, what determines an individual firm's production costs?
options: Only the firm's own output volume, with larger firms always having lower costs
The total size of the entire industry concentrated in that particular location
Government subsidies that reduce costs for all firms operating in the industry
The exchange rate between the domestic currency and foreign trading currencies
questions.explanation: External economies arise from industry-wide factors like specialized suppliers, shared labor pools, and knowledge spillovers, not from any individual firm's own scale.

questions.question: What is trade diversion in the context of regional trade agreements?
options: Creating entirely new trade flows that did not previously exist between member nations
Shifting imports from a lower-cost non-member to a higher-cost member due to preferential tariffs
Redirecting exports away from traditional partners toward faster-growing emerging markets
Relocating domestic factories to other member countries with lower corporate tax rates
questions.explanation: Trade diversion is welfare-reducing. Preferential tariff removal causes imports to shift from the globally most efficient producer to a less efficient bloc member.

questions.question: What is the net national welfare effect of an export subsidy imposed by a small country?
options: Positive, because the subsidy helps domestic firms capture foreign market share
Negative, due to production and consumption distortions plus the fiscal cost to government
Zero, because gains to producers are exactly offset by losses to consumers and taxpayers
Uncertain, depending entirely on the price elasticity of foreign import demand
questions.explanation: A small country cannot improve its terms of trade. The subsidy creates deadweight losses while costing the government revenue, producing a net welfare loss.

questions.question: Which interest group within a country would be most strongly opposed to removing an existing import quota?
options: Domestic consumers who benefit from the lower prices that free trade would bring
Domestic producers of the protected good, who would face increased foreign competition
Foreign exporters who would gain unrestricted access to the domestic market
The government, because quota removal eliminates all customs revenue collection
questions.explanation: The quota protects domestic firms from foreign competition and enables them to charge higher prices. Its removal would reduce their profits and market share.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a countervailing duty imposed on imported goods?
options: To raise general government revenue from all categories of imported merchandise
To offset the competitive advantage created by a foreign government's export subsidies
To punish foreign countries that refuse to join international trade organizations
To encourage domestic firms to increase their own exports to foreign markets
questions.explanation: Countervailing duties are designed to neutralize the price advantage foreign firms receive from their government's export subsidies, leveling the playing field.

questions.question: A large country imposes a tariff. The world price falls from \$100 to \$90 and the domestic price rises to \$105. What is the per-unit tariff amount?
options: \$5, reflecting the increase in the domestic price paid by local consumers
\$15, equal to the difference between the new domestic and new world prices
\$10, matching the decrease in the world price caused by reduced import demand
\$25, the sum of the domestic price increase and the world price decrease
questions.explanation: The tariff equals the wedge between the domestic price and the world price: \$105 – \$90 = \$15 per unit.

questions.question: Using the same tariff example (domestic price \$105, world price \$90, original world price \$100), how much of the \$15 tariff is passed through to domestic consumers?
options: \$15, because consumers always bear the full burden of any import tariff
\$5, representing the increase in the domestic price above the original world price
\$10, reflecting the portion of the tariff absorbed by foreign exporters
\$0, because in a large country tariffs are fully absorbed by foreign suppliers
questions.explanation: The domestic price rose from \$100 to \$105. Consumers pay \$5 of the \$15 tariff. Foreign exporters absorb the remaining \$10 through lower world prices.

questions.question: What defines the optimal tariff rate for a large country in international trade theory?
options: The rate that eliminates all imports and achieves complete national self-sufficiency
The tariff rate where the marginal terms-of-trade gain equals the marginal efficiency loss
Zero percent, because any positive tariff always reduces total national welfare
The maximum rate permitted under the country's World Trade Organization commitments
questions.explanation: The optimal tariff maximizes national welfare by balancing the benefit of improved terms of trade against the cost of domestic production and consumption distortions.

questions.question: What two components make up a compound tariff on an imported good?
options: A tariff on raw materials combined with a separate tariff on the finished good
Both a specific per-unit tariff and an ad valorem percentage tariff applied together
An import tariff paired with an equal export subsidy for the same product category
A low initial tariff rate that increases over time as import volumes accumulate
questions.explanation: A compound tariff combines a fixed dollar amount per unit (specific) with a percentage of the good's assessed value (ad valorem).

questions.question: A government replaces an import quota with a tariff that restricts imports by exactly the same volume. What happens to the government's fiscal position?
options: Government revenue decreases because tariffs are more costly to administer than quotas
Government revenue increases because quota rents become tariff revenue for the treasury
Government revenue remains unchanged because both policies generate the same income
Government revenue falls to zero because tariffs cannot generate any tax income
questions.explanation: The quota rent previously captured by private license holders is now collected as tariff revenue by the government, improving the fiscal position.

questions.question: What do Sanitary and Phytosanitary measures regulate in international trade?
options: The maximum quantity of goods that can be imported from any single country
Standards to protect human, animal, or plant life from pests, diseases, and contaminants
The percentage of a product's value that must be sourced from domestic suppliers
Government procurement policies that favor domestic firms over foreign bidders
questions.explanation: SPS measures are health and safety regulations. While scientifically legitimate, they can sometimes be used as disguised non-tariff barriers to restrict agricultural imports.

questions.question: How does a trade embargo compare to a standard import quota?
options: An embargo applies only to agricultural goods while quotas cover manufactured products
An embargo is simply a quota set at zero, representing a complete ban on all trade
Embargoes are imposed by the WTO while quotas are imposed by individual countries
An embargo restricts exports while a quota only restricts imports of a good
questions.explanation: An embargo is an extreme form of quota set at zero. It completely prohibits trade with a specific country or in a specific good, usually for political or security reasons.

questions.question: Which international organization serves as the primary forum for negotiating reductions in both tariffs and non-tariff barriers?
options: The International Monetary Fund, which oversees global financial stability
The World Trade Organization, which governs international trade rules and disputes
The World Bank, which provides development financing to poor nations
The Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, based in Paris
questions.explanation: The WTO provides the institutional framework for global trade negotiations, monitors trade policies, and adjudicates disputes between member countries.

questions.question: A country has a trade deficit of \$80 billion, receives \$20 billion in interest from foreign investments, and receives \$10 billion in foreign aid. What is its Current Account balance?
options: +\$30 billion, reflecting the surplus from interest and aid minus the trade deficit
–\$50 billion, the sum of the trade deficit and the net primary and secondary income
–\$110 billion, adding the negative trade balance to the positive income flows
+\$10 billion, because the aid and interest exactly offset the trade deficit
questions.explanation: CA = Trade Balance + Net Primary Income + Net Secondary Income = –\$80B + \$20B + \$10B = –\$50B.

questions.question: How is a domestic resident's purchase of a foreign government bond recorded in the country's Balance of Payments?
options: As a credit in the Current Account reflecting the purchase of a foreign asset
As a debit in the Financial Account because capital is flowing out of the country
As a credit in the Financial Account reflecting increased foreign liabilities
As a debit in the Current Account under the services and income category
questions.explanation: Buying a foreign bond is a capital outflow. The resident acquires a foreign asset, which is recorded as a debit in the Financial Account.

questions.question: A country consistently runs Current Account deficits year after year. What must eventually happen to its international investment position?
options: The country accumulates more foreign assets and its net wealth position improves
The country's Net International Investment Position declines as foreign liabilities grow
The Current Account and the NIIP are entirely unrelated and have no connection
Persistent deficits cause the currency to appreciate, improving the NIIP over time
questions.explanation: Persistent deficits must be financed by borrowing from abroad or selling existing foreign assets, which steadily increases net liabilities to the rest of the world.

questions.question: What is the correct national income identity for an open economy?
options: $Y = C + I + G$ without any additional terms for international transactions
$Y = C + I + G + EX - IM$, accounting for exports and imports of goods and services
$Y = C + I + G + T$, where T represents total tariff revenue collected at the border
$Y = C + S + T$, the closed-economy identity that excludes all trade flows
questions.explanation: In an open economy, aggregate demand includes net exports ($EX - IM$) as the foreign sector's contribution to total spending on domestic output.

questions.question: The Yen interest rate is 0.5% and the Dollar interest rate is 4.5%. The Yen is expected to appreciate by 2% against the Dollar. What is the total expected return on Yen deposits for a Dollar-based investor?
options: 4.5%, matching the higher Dollar interest rate available to US investors
2.5%, the sum of the Yen interest rate and the expected currency appreciation
0.5%, reflecting only the local currency interest rate on the Yen deposit
6.5%, combining all three: Yen interest, Dollar interest, and appreciation
questions.explanation: Total expected return = Local interest rate + Expected currency appreciation = $0.5\% + 2\% = 2.5\%$ in Dollar terms.

questions.question: The nominal exchange rate stays fixed, but the domestic price level rises faster than the foreign price level. What happens to the real exchange rate?
options: The real exchange rate depreciates, making domestic goods more competitive
The real exchange rate appreciates as domestic goods become relatively more expensive
The real exchange rate remains constant because the nominal rate has not changed
The real exchange rate is unaffected because it depends only on the nominal rate
questions.explanation: Real exchange rate $q = E \times P_{domestic} / P_{foreign}$. When $P_{domestic}$ rises faster, $q$ increases, representing a real appreciation and reduced competitiveness.

questions.question: A speculative attack against a fixed exchange rate currency typically occurs under what circumstance?
options: When the currency is strongly undervalued and expected to appreciate significantly
When investors believe the central bank lacks sufficient reserves to maintain the peg
When the country has a large and persistent trade surplus with all its partners
When interest rates in the country are significantly higher than global market rates
questions.explanation: Speculators sell the currency short, anticipating a devaluation. If reserves are inadequate, the central bank cannot defend the peg and is forced to abandon it.

questions.question: What is the core prediction of Purchasing Power Parity theory about long-run exchange rates?
options: Exchange rates are determined entirely by government monetary policy decisions
Exchange rates should adjust so that identical baskets of goods cost the same across countries
Currencies always depreciate over time because of continuous inflation in all economies
Exchange rates are unpredictable and follow a purely random statistical walk
questions.explanation: PPP is the long-run equilibrium theory of exchange rates. It predicts that exchange rates move to equalize the purchasing power of different currencies.
